1: eleh mas'ey beney Yisrael asher yatze'u me'eretz Mitzrayim: 'These are the stages of the Israelites' journey out of Egypt.' Numbers 33 is the Torah's official travel memoir — a comprehensive catalog of the 42 encampments from Egypt to the plains of Moab. The word mas'ey (stages/journeys, from nasa' = to pull up stakes and march) is the technical term for the tabernacle-moving procedure described in Num 10: YHWH's cloud leads, the priests blow trumpets, and each tribe moves in order. The entire wilderness wandering is a series of cloud-led masa-movements.
2: vayichtov Moshe et motse'eihem lemasa'eihem al pi YHWH: 'By YHWH's command, Moses recorded their departures stage by stage.' The wilderness itinerary is not a private travel journal but a divinely commanded document. Moses writes it 'by YHWH's command' — the itinerary has canonical status, not merely historical value. Every campsite and every movement from Egypt to Moab is YHWH's orchestrated journey, worth recording because it is the story of divine faithfulness through forty years of wilderness provision and discipline.
3: The departure from Rameses: 'the first month, on the fifteenth day — the day after the Passover.' The precise dating (15th of Nisan) places the Exodus in the liturgical calendar — the Passover on the 14th (Lev 23:5), the departure on the 15th. The timing is not incidental: Israel's departure from Egypt is framed as the immediate follow-through of the Passover sacrifice. The Passover lamb's blood opened the exit; the morning after, Israel marched out. 'With a high hand' (beyad ramah) is the idiom for public, triumphant, unhurried departure — the opposite of a secret escape.
4: umitzrayim meqabberim et asher hikah YHWH bahem kol bechor: 'while the Egyptians were burying their firstborn.' The juxtaposition is deliberate: while Egypt buries its firstborn, Israel marches out in triumph. The firstborn judgment (the tenth plague) is what broke Pharaoh's resistance and opened the Exodus. The burial language captures the aftermath — Egypt is occupied with grief while Israel is occupied with liberation. 'The LORD had executed judgments on their gods' — the plagues are simultaneously divine judgments on the specific Egyptian deities associated with the Nile, the sky, the sun, fertility, and death.
5: Rameses → Succoth: the first stage. Succoth (booths/shelters) may be the Egyptian site of Tell el-Maskhuta, on the eastern edge of the Nile delta. The name anticipates the Feast of Booths (Sukkot), which commemorates the wilderness sojourn (Lev 23:42-43). Israel's first campsite after Egypt is itself named for the temporary shelters that will become the primary metaphor for their wilderness existence — YHWH's people dwell in temporary shelters, pilgrims not settlers, dependent on the cloud for direction and the manna for food.
6: Succoth → Etham: on 'the edge of the wilderness.' Etham marks the boundary between the settled Nile delta region and the Sinai wilderness — the point of no return. Once Israel crosses from Etham into the wilderness, they have left the predictable provision of Egyptian agriculture for the uncertain provision of YHWH. The wilderness edge is where faith begins.
7: Etham → Pi-hahiroth: Israel 'turned back' (shav) toward Pi-hahiroth, camping between Migdol and the sea. The doubling-back before the sea is the route that set up the trap: Pharaoh could see that Israel appeared disoriented and trapped (Exod 14:3). Pi-hahiroth's exact location is uncertain; the description (between Migdol and the sea, opposite Baal-zephon) locates it somewhere on the isthmus between the Mediterranean and a body of water crossed in the sea-miracle.
8: vayaavru betoch hayam hamidbarah: 'they passed through the middle of the sea into the wilderness.' The sea-crossing is compressed to a single clause in the itinerary — the most dramatic event of the Exodus is recorded with geographic minimalism: 'through the middle of the sea.' Three days' travel in the wilderness of Etham brings them to Marah, where the waters were bitter until Moses cast a tree into them (Exod 15:23-25). The compressed record makes the sea-crossing a stage in a journey, not a one-time spectacle — YHWH's miraculous acts are the normal means of Israel's travel.
9: Marah → Elim: 'twelve springs and seventy palms.' Elim is the oasis that follows the bitter-water crisis — the gift of abundance after the trial of thirst. The specific numbers (12 springs = 12 tribes; 70 palms = 70 elders?) may be symbolic or literal; the point is that YHWH follows the test with provision. The oasis of Elim is one of the wilderness narrative's few unambiguously positive sites — no complaints, no crisis, just rest and water.
10: Elim → Red Sea encampment: Israel camps 'by the Red Sea.' This encampment on the coast of the Gulf of Suez (or Gulf of Aqaba, depending on the route theory) is not mentioned in the main Exodus-Numbers narrative. The itinerary preserves traditions of stations that the narrative did not elaborate. The mas'ot list has independent historical memory, recording movements the main narrative skipped.
11: Red Sea → wilderness of Sin: the 'Sin' wilderness (not related to the English word 'sin'; the Hebrew is Tzin or Sin) is the Sinai peninsula's central plateau, where Israel first received manna (Exod 16). The wilderness of Sin is the site of the manna-miracle — bread from heaven given to feed a people who had no agricultural land and no stores. This is one of the wilderness's defining provision events, establishing the daily-bread pattern that Jesus will recall in the Lord's Prayer.
12: Wilderness of Sin → Dophkah: Dophkah is unattested outside this itinerary — possibly the Egyptian turquoise-mining site of Serabit el-Khadim in the Sinai. Many of the intermediate stations between Sin and Sinai are known only from this list. The itinerary preserves names that may reflect local toponymy (Bedouin place-names, Egyptian administrative names, topographic features) rather than Israelite naming.
13: Dophkah → Alush: another site known only from this list, somewhere in the Sinai peninsula en route to the mountain. The itinerary's value lies partly in its preservation of stations whose stories are not told elsewhere — they represent days of ordinary wilderness travel, marked by YHWH's cloud-guidance but without recorded crisis or miracle.
14: ve'en mayim la'am lishto: 'and there was no water for the people to drink.' Rephidim is the site of the water-from-the-rock miracle (Exod 17:1-7) and the battle with Amalek (Exod 17:8-16). The itinerary notes only the water-crisis, not the miracle or the battle. The parenthetical note preserves the memory of the site's significance: Rephidim is not just a campsite but the place where Israel learned that YHWH provides even in impossible circumstances and that YHWH fights when Israel cannot.
15: Rephidim → wilderness of Sinai: the arrival at Sinai is the theological center of the entire wilderness journey. The mountain where YHWH appeared, gave the Torah, and established the covenant is the axis around which Numbers' entire narrative orbits. Israel spent approximately a year at Sinai (Exod 19:1 — Num 10:11-12) — the longest stay at any campsite. The itinerary records this as a single stage among others, but the Sinai sojourn encompasses five biblical books' worth of covenant-making.
16: Sinai → Kibroth-hattaavah ('graves of craving'): the site of the quail-plague judgment (Num 11:4-34). The name is the memorial of divine punishment — people who craved meat instead of trusting YHWH's manna provision were struck down while the meat was still in their mouths. The place-name is a standing sermon: the wilderness landscape is marked by the memory of Israel's failures as well as its triumphs.
17: Kibroth-hattaavah → Hazeroth: site of Miriam's leprosy-judgment for challenging Moses's authority (Num 12). The movement from the graves-of-craving to Hazeroth carries the narrative weight of two consecutive leadership crises: first the people's craving (Num 11), then Miriam and Aaron's challenge (Num 12). The wilderness journey is not merely geographic but spiritual — the stages map Israel's growing and failing trust in YHWH's provision and leadership.
18: Hazeroth → Rithmah: unknown site, possibly related to rothem (broom tree). The movement away from Hazeroth begins the long middle section of the itinerary — the 38 years of wilderness wandering between the spy-disaster at Kadesh and the final approach to Moab. Most of the stations in this middle section (v18-36) are unknown and unidentified; they represent the anonymous years of wilderness wandering for the generation condemned to die before the next generation enters the land.
19: Rithmah → Rimmon-perez: unknown. The name Rimmon-perez (pomegranate of the breach) may preserve memory of a specific incident at the site. The place-names throughout the wilderness journey often encode event-memories — each name is a condensed narrative. Even unknown sites carry names that once had meaning to those who camped there.
20: Rimmon-perez → Libnah: unknown, possibly 'white tree' or related to the city Libnah in the Shephelah. The itinerary's middle section moves through largely unidentifiable terrain — the wilderness of the Negev and southern Transjordan. The historical memory is preserved in names even when the narrative behind each name is lost.
21: Libnah → Rissah: unknown. Each stage represents days of march through terrain that provided no permanent landmarks, following a cloud that told them when to go and when to stop. The wilderness itinerary is fundamentally a record of obedience: Israel marched when the cloud lifted and camped when it stayed.
22: Rissah → Kehelathah: unknown, possibly 'gathering' or 'assembly place' (qahal). The name may preserve a memory of a communal gathering that took place at this site during the forty years. The names are the only windows into the otherwise undocumented middle decades of the wilderness sojourn.
23: Kehelathah → Mount Shepher: unknown, though shepher may mean 'beauty' or 'hill.' The stations in this section of the itinerary suggest a journey through the highland regions of the Negev and perhaps into the Arabah. The forty years of wandering are not simply idling — the cloud is moving, Israel is moving, and YHWH is managing the timing and direction of the whole pilgrimage.
24: Mount Shepher → Haradah: possibly 'trembling/fear,' preserving the memory of an event that caused the community to tremble. Place-names in the wilderness frequently encode the emotional register of events — fear, hunger, water-crisis, rebellion, burial — alongside purely geographic description.
25: Haradah → Makheloth: 'gatherings/assemblies.' Another name in the qahal semantic field, suggesting communal gathering events that marked the site. The assemblies of the wilderness were not always positive — some of the most significant gatherings were rebellion-assemblies (Num 14:1, 16:3). The names preserve both the form (gathering) and let the reader infer the content from the narrative context.
26: Makheloth → Tahath: 'beneath/below,' possibly a topographic description of a valley or low-lying campsite. The desert terrain of the Negev and Sinai includes dramatic elevation changes — valleys, wadis, and high plateaus. The campsites track the terrain as much as the narrative.
27: Tahath → Terah: unknown. The shared name with Abraham's father (Terach) may be coincidental topography or may preserve a later folk etymology connecting the patriarch's origin to wilderness geography. The wilderness itinerary covers terrain associated with the patriarchal narratives — Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob traveled through some of the same Negev and Arabah regions.
28: Terah → Mithkah: 'sweetness.' The name suggests a site with good water or sweet springs — a positive provision memory in the midst of the wilderness wandering. The wilderness is not uniformly harsh; YHWH's provision creates oases of sweetness along the route.
29: Mithkah → Hashmonah: unknown, possibly related to hashmon (fruitful place). The names cluster around themes of provision, gathering, fear, and geography — the full emotional range of forty years' wandering compressed into place-names.
30: Hashmonah → Moseroth: unknown, though this may be the same as Moserah where Aaron died (Deut 10:6). If so, the itinerary's sequence differs from Deut 10:6's sequence, a classic textual crux. Moseroth/Moserah is associated with Aaron's death in the Deuteronomy parallel, though Numbers locates Aaron's death at Mount Hor (v38). The variant may preserve different stages of the same journey.
31: Moseroth → Bene-jaakan: 'sons of Jaakan,' a tribe or clan name associated with wells or cisterns in the Arabah. Deut 10:6 places the Bene-jaakan stage in the context of Aaron's death — the itinerary may preserve an alternate route memory. The wilderness journey involved multiple routes and doubled-back segments; different traditions may have preserved different route sequences.
32: Bene-jaakan → Hor-haggidgad: 'the cave/pit of Gidgad,' unknown. The name suggests a rocky terrain with caves or hollows — typical of the Negev highlands and Arabah. The specific geography of each campsite would have been vivid to those who lived it and progressively obscure as the narrative passed through generations of transmission.
33: Hor-haggidgad → Jotbathah: 'a land of rushing brooks' (Deut 10:7). The itinerary's bare name (Yotvatah) is glossed in Deuteronomy as a good-water site — another provision memory encoded in a place-name. The wilderness journey includes genuine oases and life-giving sites alongside the better-known crisis-points.
34: Jotbathah → Abronah: unknown, possibly 'crossing place' from the root abar (to cross). If so, the name preserves memory of a crossing — a ford, a pass, or a transition point in the journey. The itinerary's names often capture the most memorable feature of each site: water, terrain, event, or crisis.
35: Abronah → Ezion-geber: identified with Tell el-Kheleifeh at the head of the Gulf of Aqaba. Ezion-geber is the Red Sea port where Solomon later built ships for his Ophir trade (1 Kgs 9:26). Israel's campsite at the head of the Red Sea is a significant southward excursion — the wilderness journey reached the coast before turning north again toward Kadesh. The same Ezion-geber/Elath region will be the starting point of Solomon's maritime empire.
36: Ezion-geber → wilderness of Zin (Kadesh): the itinerary explicitly identifies this as Kadesh — the central base of operations for the wilderness generation and the site of the spy-disaster. Israel arrives at Kadesh twice in the wilderness narrative: once before the spy episode (Num 13:26) and once at the beginning of the final year (Num 20:1). The itinerary's Kadesh is the second arrival — after thirty-eight years of wandering, the surviving generation returns to the edge of the promised land.
37: Kadesh → Mount Hor: the penultimate stage before Aaron's death. Mount Hor is on the border of Edom — Israel has completed the Transjordan arc and arrived at the northern frontier of Edom, the traditional territory of Esau's descendants. The location 'on the border of Edom' frames Aaron's death at the edge of the nations who contested Israel's path, a reminder that even the high priest cannot complete the journey he began.
38: vaya'al Aharon hakohen el Har haHar al pi YHWH vayamot sham bishenat ha'arba'im: 'Aaron the priest went up Mount Hor at YHWH's command and died there in the fortieth year.' The death of Aaron is the only individual death recorded with a precise date in the itinerary: 'the first day of the fifth month in the fortieth year after the Israelites left Egypt.' The precision marks it as a watershed moment: the last of the original exodus generation's leaders (after Moses and Joshua, who are still living) from the priestly line dies at the edge of the promised land. Aaron never entered what he served toward.
39: va'Aharon ben esrim ushalosh meah shanah bemoto beHar haHar: 'Aaron was 123 years old when he died at Mount Hor.' Aaron was born three years before Moses (Exod 7:7 — Aaron was 83 when he stood before Pharaoh), making him the oldest of the wilderness generation's major figures. His 123 years span from Egyptian slavery through the Exodus, covenant-making at Sinai, the golden calf, the institution of the Aaronic priesthood, and forty years of desert ministry. He dies at the edge of the land, as Moses will die at the edge of Moab.
40: The Canaanite king of Arad hears that Israel is coming — a strategic intelligence note embedded in the itinerary. Arad, in the Negev highlands, had already clashed with Israel (Num 21:1-3) when YHWH gave them victory. The repeated awareness of Israel's approach by Canaanite kings reflects the conquest narrative's military intelligence theme: the nations know YHWH is with Israel (cf. Rahab's testimony in Josh 2:9-11) and respond with fear or resistance.
41: Mount Hor → Zalmonah: after Aaron's death, the journey resumes immediately. The itinerary does not pause for extended mourning — the cloud moves, and Israel follows. The thirty-day mourning period for Aaron (Num 20:29) occurs within the Mount Hor stage; when it ends, Israel marches on. The wilderness journey's rhythm overrides even the grief of losing the high priest.
42: Zalmonah → Punon: Punon is tentatively identified with Feinan in modern Jordan, a site with ancient copper mines. The copper mines of Feinan were worked in the Bronze Age and later by Roman prisoners. Israel's campsite at an ancient industrial site is one of the few places where the wilderness journey intersects with known archaeological geography.
43: Punon → Oboth: unknown, but likely in the Arabah region. The journey is moving northward along the Arabah valley toward the Transjordan, retracing part of the route from Ezion-geber. Oboth is mentioned in Num 21:10 in the context of Israel's movements after the bronze snake incident.
44: Oboth → Iye-abarim ('ruins of the Abarim'): on Moab's border. The Abarim range is the mountain chain east of the Dead Sea from which Moses will view the promised land and on which he will die (Num 27:12; Deut 32:49). Israel's encampment at Iye-abarim marks their approach to the final stages of the wilderness journey — the Abarim highlands are both the frontier of the promised land and the place of Moses's death.
45: Iye-abarim → Dibon-gad: on the Arnon plateau, deep in what was Moabite and then Amorite territory. Dibon-gad (Dibon of Gad) anticipates the Gadite settlement of Num 32:34, where Gad rebuilds Dibon as one of its Transjordan cities. The itinerary records Israel's passage through the territory they will eventually inherit.
46: Dibon-gad → Almon-diblathaim: unknown; the name possibly combines two geographic features ('hidden' + a place). The Arnon plateau region is the area captured from Sihon in Num 21 — Israel's campsite here is in conquered territory, moving through the land YHWH has already given into their hand.
47: Almon-diblathaim → Abarim mountains before Nebo: Mount Nebo is the peak from which Moses will view the promised land (Deut 34:1) and from which he will die (Deut 34:5). The approach to Nebo is the approach to Moses's death — the itinerary places Israel in the shadow of their leader's end before the narrative records it. Nebo's geographic position (commanding view of all Canaan) makes it the place of both ultimate vision and ultimate unfulfillment: seeing but not entering.
48: Abarim mountains → plains of Moab by the Jordan. The final encampment is Israel's last wilderness stage — the plains of Moab opposite Jericho, where the Balaam episode took place (Num 22-24) and where Moses will give his final speeches (Deuteronomy). Israel has arrived at the Jordan, the crossing-point into the promised land. The itinerary that began in Egypt ends with Israel one river-crossing away from the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise.
49: 'Their camp stretched along the Jordan from Beth-jeshimoth to Abel-shittim.' The final encampment occupies a substantial stretch of the Jordan Valley — Israel's wilderness camp has reached its maximum size (the adult males of the second generation, plus families, plus the Levites). Abel-shittim ('meadow of acacias') will be remembered as the site of the Baal-peor apostasy (Num 25:1) and the final sending of the spies before the Jordan crossing (Josh 2:1; 3:1). The plains of Moab are simultaneously the end of the wilderness and the threshold of the promise.
50: The final divine speech of Numbers (v50-56) is addressed to Moses 'in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho' — the precise location of the final camp. YHWH gives conquest instructions from the exact threshold of the promised land: when you cross, do this. The instructions anticipate the Joshua narrative (the conquest described in Josh 1-12) and frame it as the continuation of the wilderness journey, not a new chapter but the completion of the journey the itinerary records.
51: ki ata'avre'u et haYarden el eretz Kena'an: 'When you cross the Jordan into Canaan.' The conditional 'when' (ki) marks the conquest instructions as the necessary follow-through of the wilderness journey. The Jordan crossing is the event the entire Numbers narrative has been building toward — the generation condemned by the spy-disaster must die; the new generation must cross. The instructions presuppose the crossing's certainty: 'when,' not 'if.'
52: vehorittem et kol yoshvey ha'aretz milifneichem: 'Drive out all the land's inhabitants.' The conquest command is total: drive out the inhabitants, destroy the carved stones (maskit), smash the cast images (tzalmey massecah), and demolish all the high places (bamot). The three categories (carved stones, cast images, high places) cover the full spectrum of Canaanite worship infrastructure — the visual media, the metal icons, and the sacred sites. The conquest is as much a religious clearance as a military campaign.
53: virishtem et ha'aretz vishavtem bah ki lachem natati et ha'aretz lareshet otah: 'Take possession of the land and live in it, for I have given it to you.' The imperative pair — yoresh (take possession) and yashav (live in) — defines the two dimensions of inheritance: legal possession (title) and actual habitation (occupation). YHWH's gift is the basis of the command: the land is given (natati, perfect tense = already accomplished fact) and Israel must take possession of what YHWH has already given. The gift precedes and grounds the command.
54: vehitnahaltem et ha'aretz begoral lemishpechotechem: 'Distribute the land by lot among your clans.' The goral (lot) is YHWH's instrument for the distribution — the allotment is not by military prowess, political negotiation, or personal preference but by divine lot. This prevents both the strong from claiming the best land and the weak from being left with nothing. The lot places the distribution explicitly in YHWH's hands: the lot's outcome is YHWH's decision (Prov 16:33). The larger clans receive larger lots; the smaller, smaller — proportionality within the divine-lot system.
55: ve'im lo torishu et yoshvey ha'aretz milifneichem vehayah asher totirun mehem lesikim be'eyneichem ulitzinim betzideiichem: 'But if you do not drive out the land's inhabitants, those you leave will be thorns in your eyes and barbs in your sides.' The warning that closes the wilderness itinerary is the warning that explains the entire book of Judges: Israel failed to drive out the Canaanites; the Canaanites became exactly the thorns and barbs YHWH described. This verse is the prospective explanation of Judges 1-2 — the subsequent history fulfills the warning verbatim.
56: vehayah ka'asher dimameti la'asot lahem e'eseh lachem: 'And what I had planned to do to them, I will do to you.' The final verse of the wilderness itinerary — and of Numbers as a whole — is the most severe warning in the book: the judgment prepared for the Canaanites will fall on Israel if they fail to complete the conquest. The symmetry is exact: the same divine action, the same judgment, turned toward the covenant people instead of the covenant people's enemies. This is the logic of covenant curse (Deut 28) applied to conquest failure. Israel does not occupy a privileged position above the law of holy war — they are subject to the same standard they are commanded to apply to Canaan.
